headline: Vivian Alma Hilden Paladin born, 1918
body:
On the fourth day of August, in the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and eighteen, amidst the vast and unfolding landscapes of Glasgow, Montana, a soul destined to chronicle the very essence of the West drew its first breath. Vivian Alma Hilden Paladin, daughter to Hans Hilden and Clara M. Haugen Hilden, emerged into a world poised on the precipice of profound change, a world that would, in time, benefit immeasurably from her meticulous gaze and unwavering dedication to the annals of history.

Her formative years, cast against the stark backdrop of the Great Depression, were not spent in idle pursuits. Even as a high school student, young Vivian found her calling within the bustling confines of The Glasgow Times. There, under the tutelage of Gordon Vallandigham, she mastered the intricate dance of the Linotype machine, learned the rhythmic feeding of the press, the precise casting of mats, and the disciplined art of finishing her work with an orderly hand. Indeed, T.J. Hocking, a former publisher for the Glasgow Courier, would later attest to her uncommon skill, remarking, "Vivian was better than an ordinary Linotype operator, first learning on an old Model One Mergenthaler." Such was her early immersion in the craft of print, a foundation that would serve her well in the grander narrative of her life.

The year nineteen hundred and thirty-nine saw Hilden depart the Courier, drawn by the siren call of higher learning to the University of Montana School of Journalism. There, she distinguished herself not merely by her intellect, but by her singular aptitude, becoming the sole woman to command the Linotype for the Montana Kaimin, the esteemed student newspaper. Her talents, once honed, were in demand, leading to offers from print shops across the burgeoning towns of Conrad, Shelby, and Cut Bank, and even extending to the distant shores of Washington state. In nineteen hundred and forty-six, she united her destiny with Jack Paladin, a U.S. Immigration Border Patrolman, whose duties would lead them, in nineteen hundred and fifty, to the vibrant metropolis of New York City. It was there, amidst the ceaseless hum of the Bergen Evening Record in Hackensack, New Jersey, that Vivian Paladin continued her invaluable work, lending her keen eye to proofreading, her discerning mind to editorial tasks, and her practiced hands to the Linotype machine.

The currents of fate, however, would draw her back to the rugged beauty of Montana in nineteen hundred and fifty-three. It was not long before her reputation preceded her, catching the attention of K. Ross Toole, then director of the Montana Historical Society. In nineteen hundred and fifty-seven, she was entrusted with the delicate task of editing a manuscript concerning the legendary Calamity Jane, a testament to her growing stature within historical circles. The following year, nineteen hundred and fifty-eight, saw her formally join the Montana Historical Society as an associate editor, a role that would soon blossom into one of profound influence.

By nineteen hundred and sixty-six, the mantle of editorship for "Montana, The Magazine of Western History" fell upon her capable shoulders. The magazine, it must be noted, had languished under previous stewardship, its fortunes waning. Yet, under Paladin's discerning eye, a renaissance began. Robert Morgan, a keen observer of the publication's trajectory, articulated the transformation with clarity: "Fortunately Vivian was named editor and when she took over in 1966 she changed the format considerably. She used illustration when they were relevant to the article and in the proper place. The former editor used illustrations as filler." He continued, with an admiration born of firsthand witness, "A good motto for Viv is tenacity over adversity. She looks out for that magazine and she doesn’t care who she tells either." This steadfast resolve, this unyielding commitment to excellence, would define her tenure.

Indeed, within a mere decade under her editorial guidance, the magazine's subscriber base burgeoned from a modest five thousand five hundred to a robust twelve thousand, a testament to her vision and tireless efforts. For eleven years, Paladin presided over the magazine, her leadership culminating in the prestigious American Association for State and Local History’s Award of Merit, a recognition of her singular achievement in elevating the publication to a nationally recognized and award-winning historical journal. Though she retired from the Montana Historical Society in nineteen hundred and seventy-eight, her wisdom continued to grace the editorial board of the magazine, a guiding light for future generations. Her earthly journey concluded on the twenty-first of May, two thousand and two, in Keller, Texas, at the venerable age of eighty-three, leaving behind a legacy etched deeply into the historical consciousness of Montana, a legacy of preservation, illumination, and an enduring passion for the stories that shape a land and its people.
